region_of_origin: South India / Tamil Nadu; served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for curry-leaf prawn dry fry specifically; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302051) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for curry-leaf marinated prawn fry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 represents prawn as a food substance; no specific concept for this preparation exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Karuveepilai Eraal Varuval is approximately 200 g, comprising 8–12 medium-to-large prawns coated in a dry curry-leaf spice paste.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn (Penaeus monodon), cooked, dry heat — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15150 (Shrimp, cooked) for micronutrients
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base prawn (cooked) values from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition, Table: Seafood). Oil absorption (~5 g fat per 100 g) and spice contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central for comparable South Indian dry prawn fry preparations. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. Sodium elevated to reflect salt and spice marinade typical of this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for cooked shrimp / seafood (negligible carbohydrate food)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Karuveepilai Eraal Varuval. Cooked prawns / shrimp are a very-low-carbohydrate food (carbohydrate_g ~2.8 per 100 g). GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous low-carbohydrate seafood entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and consistent with the principle that foods with negligible available carbohydrate have a GI near zero. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (5 × 5.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.28, rounded to 0.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1040 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 1040 mg sodium (~45% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced salt in the marinade, and balance the meal with low-sodium accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g per 100 g) and negligible glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Karuveepilai Eraal Varuval has a very low glycemic load (~0.3 per serving) and high protein content (~19.5 g per 100 g), making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, sodium intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~152 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium; offset by favourable omega-3 fatty acid profile of prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns provide beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein, but the cholesterol content (~304 mg per 200 g serving) and elevated sodium warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, particularly those managing LDL cholesterol or blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Karuveepilai Eraal Varuval is a South Indian Tamil dry prawn fry in which fresh prawns are marinated in a fragrant paste of curry leaves (karuveepilai), red chilli, turmeric, black pepper, garlic, and ginger, then stir-fried or pan-roasted in oil until the spice coating is dry and slightly crisp. The dish originates from Tamil Nadu cuisine and is widely served in South Indian restaurants across Singapore, particularly in establishments specialising in Chettinad or Tamil home-style cooking. The texture is firm and slightly chewy with a dry, aromatic spice crust; the flavour profile is intensely savoury, peppery, and herbaceous from the curry leaves, with a mild heat from chilli. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~19.5 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~8.5 g per 100 g, primarily from cooking oil), and very low in carbohydrates (~2.8 g per 100 g), yielding approximately 165 kcal per 100 g. The glycemic load per serving is negligible, making it appropriate for low-glycemic dietary patterns. Sodium is elevated (~520 mg per 100 g) due to salt in the marinade, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Prawns are a source of selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared with permissible ingredients.
common_user_queries: Is curry leaf prawn fry healthy?
How many calories in prawn varuval?
Is eraal varuval good for diabetics?
Is prawn fry high in sodium?
South Indian prawn dry fry recipe

ingredient_synonyms.curry leaves: Murraya koenigii, sweet neem leaves, kadi patta

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Karuveepilai Eraal Varuval exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data with estimated oil absorption and spice contribution consistent with South Indian dry fry preparations. GI is an analogous estimate based on the negligible carbohydrate content of cooked prawns per University of Sydney GI Database principles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (cooked prawn baseline) with an estimated ~5 g/100 g fat addition for cooking oil absorption typical of South Indian dry fry preparations; values should be verified against a laboratory analysis of the specific dish if high-precision data are required. 2. Sodium (~520 mg/100 g) is an estimate reflecting salt in the marinade and cooking; actual sodium will vary by restaurant recipe. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as prawns contain negligible trans fats and no hydrogenated oils are used in this preparation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central shrimp entries. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN seafood tables for this preparation. 5. GI is set to 5 as an analogous estimate for a very-low-carbohydrate seafood dish; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status assumes preparation with halal-certified prawns and permissible oil; kosher status is false as shellfish are not kosher. 7. The field dataed (Manam - Upper Circular Road, field survey data) serves this dish at SGD 25.00; price data preserved from input but not used as a nutritional evidence source.
